does: This paper evaluates whether psychometric self-reports from large language models predict their actual behavior, comparing two assessment frameworks. The authors administer Big Five personality tests and Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) questionnaires to 11 frontier LLMs, then measure behavioral alignment across four behavioral tasks while varying conversational context and identity induction. They find that TPB measures achieve human-level coherence between self-reports and behavior within shared conversations, whereas Big Five personality traits show weak prediction. Crucially, coherence across separate conversations persists selectively for certain behaviors, suggesting LLMs exhibit meaningful but context-dependent consistency rather than wholesale dissociation between stated intentions and actions.
connects: This work responds directly to recent findings documenting significant gaps between LLM self-reports and actual behavior, questioning whether these dissociations reflect genuine behavioral incoherence or methodological limitations. The paper builds on decades of psychometric research in human psychology showing that broad trait measures like Big Five poorly predict specific behaviors, while targeted intention frameworks like Theory of Planned Behavior achieve substantially better prediction. The authors extend this human-validated insight to LLMs, leveraging TPB's specificity advantage rather than assuming broad personality traits would transfer directly. Their systematic variation of conversational context and identity induction responds to prior work that isolated behavioral sessions, addressing whether apparent coherence loss stems from genuine LLM limitations or experimental design.
enables: These findings establish a methodological framework for more reliable behavioral prediction of LLMs prior to deployment, suggesting that targeted intention measures can serve as valid low-cost safety probes when properly designed. The selective coherence results point toward understanding which behavioral domains support genuine LLM consistency and which remain context-dependent, enabling more nuanced risk assessment. This work opens questions about the mechanistic basis for TPB coherence in LLMs and how to design psychometric instruments that capture behavioral stability across deployment contexts. The results also suggest possibilities for improving behavioral alignment through better-targeted prompting or training approaches that leverage behaviors showing natural coherence, while flagging high-risk domains where self-reports and behavior diverge predictably.
